coredata.dc:description: The goal of this study is to explore the synthesis and potential antibacterial and wound healing properties of carbon quantum dots embedded silver nanoparticles (CQDs@AgNPs)-based nanocomposites, which demonstrate great promise for biomedical applications. A simple and eco-friendly one-step chemical reduction process was employed, with CQDs acting as both reducing and stabilizing agents. Comprehensive physiochemical characterization, including X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and Zeta potential analysis, confirmed the successful formation of the CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites. The synergistic integration of CQDs and AgNPs resulted in enhanced antimicrobial efficacy against both Gram-negative [Escherichia coli (E. coli), Klebsiella pneumonia (K. pneumonia), and Acinetobacter baumannii (A. baumannii)] and Gram-positive [Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus)], and the fungal species Candida albicans (C. albicans), as demonstrated through in vitro assays. The CQDs@AgNPs-based nanocomposites demonstrated a strong minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of 0.117 mg/mL for E. coli, 3.75 mg/mL for K. pneumoniae, A. baumannii, and S. aureus, and MIC of 15 mg/mL for C. albicans. The minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) results mirrored the MIC values, indicating that the nanocomposites have a bactericidal effect against E. coli, K. pneumoniae, and A. baumannii, while exhibiting a bacteriostatic effect against S. aureus and C. albicans. Importantly, the CQDs@AgNPs-based nanocomposites demonstrated excellent antioxidant activity toward DPPH, with a radical scavenging capacity of 63,90%. In vivo studies in a mouse model highlighted remarkable wound healing properties, showing faster re-epithelialization and reduced inflammation compared to untreated wounds and those treated with standalone CQDs, AgNPs, or Povidone iodine. The combination of broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity and effective wound healing capabilities positions the CQDs@AgNPs-based nanocomposites as a promising candidate for the development of advanced antimicrobial wound healing agents. This study's findings underscore the versatility and efficacy of this eco-friendly, green-synthesized multifunctional CQDs@AgNPs-based nanocomposites.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The goal of this study is to explore the synthesis and potential antibacterial and wound healing properties of carbon quantum dots embedded silver nanoparticles (CQDs@AgNPs)-based nanocomposites, which demonstrate great promise for biomedical applications. A simple and eco-friendly one-step chemical reduction process was employed, with CQDs acting as both reducing and stabilizing agents. Comprehensive physiochemical characterization, including X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and Zeta potential analysis, confirmed the successful formation of the CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites. The synergistic integration of CQDs and AgNPs resulted in enhanced antimicrobial efficacy against both Gram-negative [Escherichia coli (E. coli), Klebsiella pneumonia (K. pneumonia), and Acinetobacter baumannii (A. baumannii)] and Gram-positive [Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus)], and the fungal species Candida albicans (C. albicans), as demonstrated through in vitro assays. The CQDs@AgNPs-based nanocomposites demonstrated a strong minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of 0.117 mg/mL for E. coli, 3.75 mg/mL for K. pneumoniae, A. baumannii, and S. aureus, and MIC of 15 mg/mL for C. albicans. The minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) results mirrored the MIC values, indicating that the nanocomposites have a bactericidal effect against E. coli, K. pneumoniae, and A. baumannii, while exhibiting a bacteriostatic effect against S. aureus and C. albicans. Importantly, the CQDs@AgNPs-based nanocomposites demonstrated excellent antioxidant activity toward DPPH, with a radical scavenging capacity of 63,90%. In vivo studies in a mouse model highlighted remarkable wound healing properties, showing faster re-epithelialization and reduced inflammation compared to untreated wounds and those treated with standalone CQDs, AgNPs, or Povidone iodine. The combination of broad-spectrum antimicrobial activity and effective wound healing capabilities positions the CQDs@AgNPs-based nanocomposites as a promising candidate for the development of advanced antimicrobial wound healing agents. This study's findings underscore the versatility and efficacy of this eco-friendly, green-synthesized multifunctional CQDs@AgNPs-based nanocomposites.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Wound management remains a significant global challenge, imposing a substantial financial burden on healthcare systems worldwide [1,2]. The prevalence of wounds has steadily increased over the past decade [3,4]. Currently, it is estimated that approximately 8.2 million people globally are affected by wounds, with or without infections, leading to medication expenses between USD 28.1 billion and USD 96.8 billion [5]. A wound is generally defined as an injury to the skin or tissue caused by external factors or trauma, resulting in cuts, punctures, or tears [6]. Daily activities often expose individuals to various skin injuries that compromise the skin's protective function, leading to wound formation [7]. Wound healing is a highly intricate and essential biological process that relies on the delicate interplay of multiple factors. It is traditionally divided into four sequential but overlapping stages: hemostasis (minutes to hours following the injury), inflammation (days 1–3), proliferation and repair (days 4–21), and remodeling (days 21–365) [8,9]. However, tissue damage significantly increases the risk of microbial infections, particularly during surgical procedures. These infections can result in inflammation, tissue necrosis, and delayed wound healing [10]. Bacteria such as Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) are particularly problematic, as they can damage healthy tissue [11]. In some cases, the body's immune system may be unable to effectively combat these bacteria, which can form biofilms at the wound site. These biofilms further impede the wound healing process, making the bacteria more difficult to eradicate [12]. Given the critical role of the skin in both physical and mental well-being, optimal wound care is essential. The primary goal of wound care is to restore the damaged skin's structure, appearance, and functionality [13]. Consequently, the development of advanced antimicrobial materials is critical to addressing these issues.

Carbon-based nanomaterials, including graphene, graphene oxide (GO), reduced graphene oxide (rGO), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs), graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), and carbon nanotubes (CNTs), have garnered significant interest due to their antimicrobial properties [11,14–17]. Among these, CQDs have recently emerged as promising tools for antibacterial and wound healing applications [17–19]. Studies have highlighted their effective antimicrobial properties and their ability to enhance the wound healing process [12,20]. Their surface charge plays a crucial role in their bactericidal activity by disrupting cell membrane integrity [21]. Bing and colleagues found that both positively and negatively charged CQDs effectively killed E. coli [22]. The primary mechanism for bacterial growth inhibition involved the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), including hydroxyl radicals [23]. Furthermore, the ability of CQDs to produce ROS facilitates the oxidation and destabilization of extracellular polymeric substances (EPS), which are essential for biofilm stability and bacterial attachment. By impairing EPS integrity and interfering with quorum sensing mechanism, CQDs significantly hinder biofilm maturation and viability [19].

CQDs are novel modified quantum dots known for their exceptional biocompatibility, non-toxicity, and antibacterial properties [24–26]. They exhibit excellent dispersion, water solubility, cost-effectiveness, and eco-friendliness, making them highly suitable for various biomedical applications [27,28]. Due to their small size (ranging from a few nanometers to 10 nm), CQDs are particularly valuable for targeted therapy applications, serving as effective carriers for nanoparticles or drugs. Their non-toxic nature has enabled their use as mitochondrial trackers, wound healing agents, and drug delivery vehicles [24,29]. CQDs can be synthesized from natural sources, such as watermelon peels, through innovative 'waste-derived' routes [30,31]. This approach not only addresses the issue of agricultural waste but also provides cost-effective carbon source rich in diverse functional groups, which enhances its capacity to attract cationic ions. The use of watermelon peels in the synthesis of nanocomposites is gaining traction due to their affordability and potential to mitigate waste. Moreover, this sustainable process could offer an environmentally friendly alternative to conventional methods by reducing the need for synthetic precursors and minimizing waste generation. From an economic perspective, utilizing waste materials like watermelon peels could lower production costs, while contributing to waste reduction strategies. However, scalability for industrial production must be considered, particularly regarding the supply chain challenges associated with sourcing sufficient quantities of watermelon waste. Watermelon peels are abundant in proteins, carotenoids, cellulose, and flavonoids, all of which contribute to improved ion interaction [32]. Furthermore, the antioxidant properties of citrulline promote the generation of hydroxyl radicals [33], facilitating effective synthesis processes and potentially aiding in wound healing.

CQDs have also demonstrated the ability to stabilize and reduce metal nanomaterials due to the abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, and epoxy) on their surface, which act as excellent electron donors and acceptors [34,35]. CQDs have been specifically chosen as reducing and stabilizing agents for the synthesis of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). The exceptional electron-donating ability of photo-excited CQDs facilitates the rapid reduction of Ag+ ions to AgNPs on their surface. This process leads to the emergence and swift enhancement of surface plasmon absorption [36]. This approach aims to enhance the antibacterial of the resulting nanocomposite.

AgNPs are widely recognized for their broad-spectrum antibacterial activity against a variety of bacteria and viruses [37]. Compared to other antibacterial materials, such as quaternary ammonium compounds, metal ions, and antibiotics, AgNPs are cost-effective, hydrophilic, and exert their antibacterial effects through multiple mechanisms, including membrane penetration, DNA damage, and disruption of cell metabolisms by inducing ROS [37,38]. The medical applications of AgNPs, including wound dressing, implant coating, and drug carries, have increased due to their potent antimicrobial properties. However, the high specific surface area and reactivity of bare AgNPs can lead to aggregation and oxidation, compromising their antibacterial efficacy. This limitation often necessitates the use off high dosages to effectively combat multidrug-resistant bacteria. To overcome these challenges, various modifications such as matrix anchoring and coating layers have been explored to improve the antibacterial activity of AgNPs [38]. By incorporating fluorescent CQDs into silver nanoparticles, the negative surface charge and hydrophilicity can be significantly increased. Moreover, the proximity between the CQDs and AgNPs allows for enhanced energy transfer, resulting in a synergetic effect that can accelerate wound closure and make the CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites highly suitable for wound healing and antibacterial applications.

The primary goal of this research is to introduce a novel and efficient method to simplify the synthesis process of CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites through a straightforward chemical reduction approach while enhancing their antibacterial, antioxidant, and wound healing properties. The prepared nanocomposites were thoroughly characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and Zeta potential analysis. Both in vivo and in vitro results demonstrated excellent wound healing properties compared to the commercial Povidone Iodine, as confirmed by histological analysis, alongside significantly enhanced antibacterial activity against a broad spectrum of pathogens, including Gram-negative bacteria [e.g., Escherichia coli (E. coli), Klebsiella pneumonia (K. pneumonia), and Acinetobacter baumannii (A. baumannii)] and Gram-positive bacteria Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), and the fungus Candida albicans (C. albicans). Notably, the CQD@AgNPs-based nanocomposites outperformed individual CQDs and AgNPs, displaying excellent minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) values and strong bactericidal effects in minimum bactericidal concentration (MBC) tests. Furthermore, the nanocomposites exhibited good antioxidant activity toward 2,2-diphényl 1-picrylhydrazyle (DPPH), further enhancing their therapeutic potential. This study highlights the promise of CQDs@AgNPs as a therapeutic agent in antibacterial and wound healing applications, paving the way for further research and development in nanomedicine.

Silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.8–100.5%), Sodium borohydride (NaBH4, ≥96%), methanol (CH3OH, ≥99.9%), diethyl ether ((CH3CH2)2O, ≥99.0 %), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), and DPPH were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). Fresh watermelon was purchased from a local store in Tetouan (Morocco).

CQDs were synthesized using a green synthesis method based on dried watermelon peels via a hydrothermal process. Initially, watermelon peels were collected, cut into smaller pieces, and thoroughly washed with deionized (DI) water. The cleaned peels were then dried in an electric oven at 65 °C for 24 h. Following this, 1.2 g of the dried peels were mixed with 120 mL of DI water and transferred to a 250 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The mixture was heated at 200 °C for 6 h. Key synthesis parameters, such as temperature, pressure, and reaction time, significantly influenced the CQDs properties. Higher temperature enhanced carbonization, resulting in smaller, more uniform CQDs, while pressure improved stability and yield. Longer reaction times facilitated better interaction between carbon source and solvent, leading to improved optical properties and size control. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting brownish-yellow solution was filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane filter to eliminate any undissolved particles. The filtered solution underwent centrifugation at 500 rpm for 15 min to separate residual solids, resulting in a clear supernatant abundant in CQDs. This sustainable approach not only repurposes waste materials but also reduces the use of harmful chemicals, underscoring the environmental benefits of our method.

AgNPs were synthesized using a simple chemical reduction method. Specifically, 40 mL of an aqueous AgNO3 solution was added dropwise to 100 mL of a NaBH4 solution while continuously stirring for 2 h. During this period, the solution gradually changed from an initial greenish-black color to yellow, indicating the successful formation of AgNPs. The resulting AgNPs solution was then washed several times with distilled water, filtered, and transferred to an oven to dry overnight, yielding a gray-colored AgNPs powder as the final product.

CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites were prepared by adding 40 mL of CQDs solution drop by drop to 40 mL of AgNO3 solution and stirred for 30 min. The mixture was then added dropwise to 60 mL of NaBH4 solution and stirred for 2 h. Finally, the resulting solution was washed multiple times with distilled water to remove any impurities, filtered, and dried in an oven overnight. In this study, the synthesis of CQDs@AgNPs using watermelon peels presents a cost-effective and sustainable approach by repurposing agricultural waste (Schema 1 ). In contrast, Liang et al. synthesized CQDs using citric acid as a precursor [39], which typically incurs higher costs associated with chemical procurement. Furthermore, CQDs derived from watermelon peels can produce nanocomposites with comparable or even superior properties, underscoring the advantages of utilizing natural waste materials in nanotechnology applications. To assess the reproducibility of the synthesis process, experiments were conducted using three separate batches of the synthesized materials, all prepared under identical conditions as outlined in the Methods section. The results from these batches demonstrated consistent performance across all samples, suggesting a reliable synthesis process.

To characterize the crystalline structure of CQDs, AgNPs, and CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites, ambient temperature XRD patterns were performed. A BRUKER-binary V4 X-Ray diffractometer with CuKα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) was used to collect the XRD patterns. All data were collected in Bragg-Brentano geometry over 2θ range from 5° to 65°, with the operation voltage and current maintained at 40 kV and 25 mA, respectively. FT-IR spectroscopy was employed to analyze the functional groups present in the samples. The spectra were recorded using a PerkinElmer FT-IR spectrometer over a wavenumber range of 4000 to 400 cm−1, providing insights into the chemical bonds and interactions between the CQDs and AgNPs in the nanocomposites. The surface morphology of the nanostructures was investigated using SEM (Zeiss Sigma 500 VP Analytical FE-SEM, Inspect Carl Zeiss Company, Germany) allowing for a detailed visualization of the particle size and distribution. Furthermore, the zeta potential of the samples was investigated using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS. which provided information on the stability and surface charge properties of the nanocomposites. This comprehensive characterization approach enhances the understanding of the structural and functional aspects of the synthesized materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial effect of CQDs, AgNPs, CQDs@AgNPs

ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial properties of the CQDs, AgNPs, and CQDs@AgNPs were evaluated against a variety of microorganisms, including E. coli, K. pneumoniae, A. baumannii, S. aureus, and C. albicans. The selection of these strains is justified by their frequent involvement in dermatological infections and their global prevalence. E. coli, K. pneumoniae, and A. baumannii are known to cause significant healthcare-associated infections, while S. aureus is a common pathogen in skin infections. Additionally, C. albicans is also a major contributor to cutaneous fungal infections [40]. By studying these specific strains, we aim to address critical challenges in treating infections that pose a risk to public health worldwide, thereby underscoring their relevance in global healthcare contexts. The bacterial strains used in this research were obtained from the American Type Culture Collection (ATCC) and the Provincial Hospital of Tetouan-Morocco (PHTM), both of which are recognized sources for strains associated with various dermatological pathologies.

The antibacterial activity of CQDs, AgNPs, and CQDs@AgNPs was evaluated using the disk diffusion method on Mueller-Hinton (MH) agar plates. Following the aromatogram method developed by Vincent [41], bacteria cultures were inoculated onto the agar surface using a cotton swab. Wells with a diameter of 6 mm were punched into the agar, and CQDs, AgNPs, as well as CQDs@AgNPs-based nanocomposites, were added to their respective wells. The plates were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h, and antibacterial activity was evaluated by measuring the diameters of the inhibition zones formed around the wells containing the materials [15].

The MIC and MBC of CQDs, AgNPs and CQDs@AgNP-based nanocomposites were evaluated using the microtiter plate method in conjunction with the MTT assay. In this procedure, 100 μL of MH broth and 100 μL of each nanomaterial were mixed and serially diluted to obtain the following concentrations: 15, 7.5, 3.75, 1.875, 0.937, 0.468, 0.234, and 0.117 mg/mL. Subsequently, 10 μL of bacterial suspension was inoculated into each well, and the microplate was incubated at 37 °C for 18 h. After incubation, 10 μL of MTT solution was added to each well, and the plate was re-incubated for an additional 2 h at 37 °C. The development of a blue-violet color in the wells indicated active microbial growth. The MBC test involves taking samples from the MIC test and inoculating them into agar plates.

The antioxidant activities of the AgNPs and CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites were evaluated using DPPH free radical scavenging assay. This assay is based on the ability of DPPH, a stable free radical, to be quenched and thereby decolorized in the presence of antioxidants, resulting in a reduction in absorbance values [42]. In the protocol, 1 mL of freshly prepared 0.1 mM DPPH solution was added to 3 mL of a methanolic solution containing the nanomaterials at concentrations ranging from 31.25 to 1000 μg/mL. After thorough mixing, the decrease in absorbance was measured at 517 nm, 30 min after incubation at room temperature in the dark, using UV–Visible spectrophotometer. The results were compared to DPPH without any sample as a control. The experiment was replicated three times to ensure precision and reliability. The DPPH radical scavenging activity was calculated using Equation (1) [43].

Where, Asample is the absorbance of the sample and Acontrol is the absorbance of the control. The IC50 value, which represents the concentration required to scavenge 50% of the DPPH radicals, was determined through analysis of the logarithmic dose inhibition curve.

For this study, healthy adult male mice aged 12–15 weeks and weighing 35–40 g were housed individually under standard laboratory conditions. This included access to standard mouse show and drinking water, a 12-h light/dark cycle, and a temperature of 23 ± 1 °C. The animals were housed in the central animal facility at Faculty of sciences, Abdelmalek Essaadi University, Tetouan, Morocco. The mice were randomly divided into five treatment groups: Incision-only control group (Untreated group), Incision treated with povidone iodine, incision treated with AgNPs, incision treated with CQDs, and incision treated with CQDs@AgNPs-based nanocomposites. All procedures were conducted in compliance with European Regulation of 1 February 2013 on the ethical assessment and approval of projects involving the use of animals in experimental procedures (NOR: AGRG1238767A). It is worth mentioning that the sample size for each group was modest. While this may influence the statistical robustness of the findings and their generalizability, we took care to minimize the number of mice used while ensuring their well-being.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental design, treatment, and toxicity analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of AgNPs, CQDs and CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites on wound healing progression was assessed using an in vivo mouse incisional wound model. Prior to the injury, the mice were anesthetized using diethyl ether. The dorsal hair was shaved, and a 1.2 cm incisional wound was created under aseptic conditions to minimize the risk of infection. Immediately after injury was inflicted, the wounds were treated. Each of the AgNPs, CQDs, and CQDs@AgNPs-based nanocomposites were prepared by dissolving the materials in DI water to achieve a concentration of 100 μg/mL [15]. A specific volume of each solution was then applied topically to the incisional wounds of the corresponding treatment groups once a day. This regimen was chosen based on preliminary studies that indicated optimal wound healing properties without adverse effects on the surrounding tissues. Furthermore, it is imperative to assess whether this concentration is optimal for human applications and to explore its scalability for clinical use. Future investigations should prioritize the determination of the ideal dosage and application methodologies to enhance the translational potential of these nanocomposites in advanced wound healing therapies. The animals were housed individually to prevent interactions that could affect the healing process. Daily observations were meticulously conducted to monitor the healing progression. These observations included the treatment of wounds to maintain cleanliness, precise measurements of wound size to quantify healing, and photographic documentation to visually capture the healing stages.

The safety and toxicity assessments were conducted through histological examination. The biotoxicity of CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites at two concentrations (0.10 and 20 mg/mL) was evaluated in each group by analyzing kidney and liver samples using H&E staining [15] (Fig. 1 S, Supplementary Material), no structural abnormalities were detected in renal tissue at the highest concentration (20 mg/mL), with tubules appearing intact. The glomeruli maintained normal morphology without capsular space obliteration or Bowman's space reduction. Similarly, liver histology after exposure to 20 mg/mL CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites showed no evidence of toxicity or tissue disorganization (Table S1 and Fig. 1S).

To evaluate the histological changes during the wound healing process, skin samples were collected at specific experimental endpoints: days 1, 3, 6 and 9. Prior to tissue collection, the mice were euthanized, and their skin tissues were harvested carefully from the dorsal region and fixed in 10% formalin for a duration of 24 h to preserve cellular structure. after fixation, the tissues were placed in plastic cassettes for processing. The samples underwent paraffin embedding to facilitate sectioning. Using a microtome, tissue sections were cut to a uniform thickness of 4 μm. For histopathological examination, the skin sections were stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) according to established protocols to highlight cellular details and tissue architecture. Imaging of the stained sections was performed using an Olympus BX41 optical microscope which was coupled to a Sony DXC 390p camera for high-resolution visualization [15]. The wound area was measured using ImageJ software. The percentage of wound healing on the specific experimental days, relative to the initial (day 0) wound size, was calculated using the following Equation (2) [44].

Statistical analyses were performed to compare healing rates among the different treatment groups, providing insights into the efficacy of each nanocomposite in promoting wound healing. The data were analyzed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) to assess differences among the groups. Following this, Tukey's post hoc test was employed to identify specific differences between group means. The results are presented as the mean ± standard deviation (SD) for each group, with statistical significance set at a p-value less than 0.05. Table S2 and S3 present detailed wound contraction data, including standard deviations and p-values for each group comparison. All analyses were performed using SPSS version 10.0 software for Windows (SPSS, IBM, Chicago, IL, USA).

The characterization of CQDs, AgNPs, and CQDs@AgNPs-based nanocomposites provided valuable insights into their structural and functional properties. XRD analysis was performed to evaluate the crystalline structure of the synthesized CQDs, AgNPs, and CQDs@AgNPs-based nanocomposites. The XRD spectrum of the prepared CQDs, shown in Fig. 1A, confirms their polycrystalline nature, with a broad diffraction peak centered around 2θ = 23°. This peak is attributed to the presence of amorphous carbon-based core-shell materials [36,45]. The spectrum also shows additional peaks corresponding to the (002), (100), (102), and (103) crystal planes, which may be associated with graphite and diamond structures in the carbon material [46]. The XRD pattern of the AgNPs shows three distinct diffraction peaks at 38.05°, 44.25°, and 64.35°, corresponding to the (111), (200), and (220) planes of face centered cubic silver (Fig. 1B). This data aligns with the Joint Committee on Powder Diffraction Standards (JCPDS) file No. 89.3722 for silver [47]. The XRD patterns of the CQDs@AgNPs-based nanocomposites, presented in Fig. 1C, display four distinct peaks located at 31.7°, 38.05°, 44.25°, and 64.35°, corresponding to the (122), (111), (200), and (220) crystallographic planes of the face-centered cubic structure of silver crystals [48]. These findings confirm the presence of AgNPs in the nanocomposites. XRD operates by directing X-rays towards the sample and measuring the angles at which they are diffracted, producing unique patterns that are indicative of different materials. Additionally, the nanocomposites exhibited a peak slightly shifted to 27.35°, which can be attributed to the (002) plane of CQDs, along with a change in peak intensities. This shift suggests a significant interaction between the AgNPs and the surface of the CQDs, which may enhance the stability and performance of the composite material. The interactions indicated by the XRD data imply that the coupling of CQDs and AgNPs could lead to synergistic effects, thereby improving the optical and catalytic properties of the nanocomposites [49,50]. In summary, the XRD results confirm the successful incorporation of both CQDs and AgNPs in the CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites material, demonstrating the effectiveness of this technique in analyzing the composition of the material.

FTIR analysis was conducted to characterize the structural and compositional features of the synthesized CQDs, AgNPs, and the CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites. The FTIR spectrum of CQDs and CQDs@AgNPs-based nanocomposites (Fig. 2 A and C) showed an absorption band at 3340 cm−1, attributed to O–H stretching vibrations [50], which is significant for interactions with biological molecules, enhancing biocompatibility. In the spectrum of CQD@AgNPs-based nanocomposites, a peak at 2931 cm−1 corresponding to C–H stretching was observed, though it was less intense than in the CQDs spectrum, suggesting interactions between CQDs and AgNPs [36,50]. The band at 1598 cm−1 was associated with C=O bending vibrations, while the peak at 1037 cm−1 was attributed to C–O–C functionalities, indicates the presence of ether groups that could contribute to the stability of the nanocomposites in physiological conditions. Additionally, the bands at 1336 and 1253 cm−1 indicated symmetric stretching vibrations of the COO− group [51]. A peak at 819 cm−1 was attributed to the C=C functional group [52]. In contrast, the FTIR spectrum of AgNPs (Fig. 2B) appeared as a simple straight line without distinct peaks, indicating the absence of organic groups on the surface of the nanoparticles. These findings highlight how the synthesis conditions, including temperature and precursor concentrations, influence the functional groups present in CQDs. The observed variations in peak intensity and position reflect the interactions between CQDs and AgNPs, which may alter the surface chemistry and enhance the nanocomposites' potential for applications in wound healing. Understanding these relationships is crucial for optimizing the properties of CQDs for therapeutic use.

The UV–Vis spectra for CQDs, AgNPs, and their nanocomposites (CQDs@AgNPs) are illustrated in Fig. 3 . The spectrum for CQDs (Fig. 3A) features a significant absorption peak at 280 nm that extends into the visible spectrum. This peak is linked to π-π∗ electronic transitions occurring in the aromatic –C=C– bonds of the sp2 hybridized graphitic core [53]. Moreover, the brown-yellow CQD solution exhibits a vivid blue luminescence under ultraviolet light, showcasing its remarkable photoluminescent properties. The UV–Vis spectrum of the synthesized AgNPs (Fig. 3B) displays a strong peak at 417 nm, indicative of the surface plasmon resonance band, confirming their successful synthesis [54]. In these nanoparticles, the overlap of the conduction and valence bands allows for the free movement of electrons, contributing to the plasmon resonance absorption [55]. In the spectrum of the CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites (Fig. 3C), a peak is observed that aligns with that of the AgNPs, though with a variation in intensity, with absence of peak for the CQDs. This absence likely results from the prominence of the AgNPs peak, which may mask any contributions from the CQDs.

The surface charges and stability of the synthesized AgNPs, CQDs, and CQD@AgNPs-based nanocomposites were assessed using zeta potential measurements. The data revealed distinct differences in the surface charge characteristics of these nanomaterials. As shown in Fig. 4 B, the zeta potential of the bare AgNPs was measured at −7.5 mV, indicating a negative surface charge [56]. In contrast, the standalone CQDs exhibited a zeta potential value of +19.23 mV (Fig. 4A), while the CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites presented a slightly lower but still positive zeta potential of +4.61 mV (Fig. 4C). These variations in surface charge reflect differences in the surface chemistry and colloidal stability of the individual nanomaterials compared to the hybrid CQDs@AgNPs system.

The SEM images in Fig. 5 illustrates the morphology of the AgNPs, CQDs, and CQDs@AgNPs-based nanocomposites. The SEM images of the CQDs in Fig. 5A indicates that CQDs have a near spherical shape. As shown in Fig. 5B, the AgNPs exhibit a relatively spherical shape, consistent with the findings reported by Ganet al. [57]. Fig. 5C reveals that the AgNPs are attached to the surface of the CQDs, forming a ball like structure. The crystalline AgNPs are observed to be well-intermixed with the CQDs nanoparticles, as reported in Ref. [58].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial effect and antimicrobial mechanism of samples
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial behaviour of the CQDs, AgNPs, and CQDs@AgNPs nanomaterials against Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria was examined using the disk diffusion method on MH agar plates. The diameter of the resulting inhibition zones serves as an indicator of the antibacterial activity, with larger diameters reflecting greater efficacy. The MIC for each nanomaterial was determined using the microtiter plate method in conjunction with the MTT assay. Inhibition zone diameters are illustrated in Fig. 6 A, with comparative values presented in Fig. 6B. MIC and MBC values of each nanomaterial are summarized in Table 1 .

As depicted in Fig. 6A and B, CQDs exhibited limited antibacterial activity, significantly inhibiting only E. coli with a 15 mm inhibition zone, indicating that CQDs alone do not possess strong enough antibacterial properties. In contrast, AgNPs displayed antibacterial effects against E. coli, S. aureus, A. baumannii, with inhibition zone diameters of 10 mm, 9 mm, and 9 mm, respectively. Notably, the CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites demonstrated broad-spectrum antibacterial activity, effectively inhibiting the growth of both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, as well as the fungal species C. albicans. Inhibition zone measurements included 15 mm against C. albicans, 13 mm against E. coli, 12 mm against S. aureus, 12 mm against A. baumannii, and 10 mm against K. pneumonia, all significantly surpassing those of AgNPs alone.

The results indicate that the Gram-negative bacteria strains were more susceptible to CQDs@AgNPs compared to Gram-positive strains, which is in agreement with previously reported findings [59]. The variation in antibacterial mechanisms across bacterial species may be attributed to differences in cell wall structures. Previous studies have found that the thicker cell wall of Gram-positive bacteria present a barrier that hinders the diffusion of AgNPs and silver ions, whereas, Gram-negative bacteria possess a thinner peptidoglycan layer that can be penetrated by ROS, making them more vulnerable to disruption by these nanomaterials [60].

The surface charge of the nanomaterials is critical in determining their antibacterial mechanisms [61]. Zeta potentials measurements revealed that CQDs and CQDs@AgNPs exhibited positive zeta potentials of +19.23 mV and +4.61 mV respectively, whereas AgNPs had a negative zeta potential of −7.5 mV. The positive surface charges of CQDs and CQDs@AgNPs facilitate stronger electrostatic interactions with negatively charged bacterial cell membranes compared to the negatively charged AgNPs [62]. The greater positive zeta potential of CQDs suggests enhanced electrostatic attraction to E. coli cell membrane, potentially contributing to the larger 15 mm inhibition zone observed for CQDs compared to the 13 mm zone for the CQDs@AgNPs. Conversely, the negatively charged AgNPs demonstrated weaker electrostatic interactions, resulting in a smaller 10 mm inhibition zone. This electrostatic interaction is believed to be a key mechanism through which CQDs@AgNPs exerts thier potent antibacterial effects, leading to bacterial cell disruption and death. Moreover, the immune responses elicited by the positive zeta potential of CQDs@AgNPs warrant consideration. The surface charge of nanomaterials significantly influences their biocompatibility and interactions with biological systems. The positive zeta potentials of both CQDs and CQDs@AgNPs enhance electrostatic interactions with negatively charged components of cell membranes, including immune cells, which may lead to unintended immune activation or inflammatory responses. In contrast, negatively charged AgNPs may exhibit weaker interactions, potentially resulting in reduced immune recognition. While the positive surface charge enhances antibacterial activity, it raises concerns regarding biocompatibility and immune responses, necessitating further investigation into the implications of zeta potential on immune interactions and overall biocompatibility.

The MIC study yielded intriguing insights into the antimicrobial potency of the different nanoparticle formulations. As shown in Table 1, the MIC value of CQDs alone was 0.468 mg/mL, indicating a relatively low concentration sufficient to inhibit E. coli growth. In contrast, AgNPs required a higher concentration, with an MIC value of 3.75 mg/mL. Remarkably, the combination of CQDs and AgNPs in CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites resulted in a significantly reduced MIC of 0.117 mg/mL, demonstrating a synergistic effect that enhances antimicrobial efficacy. MBC analysis revealed that the CQDs exhibited a bacteriostatic mode of action. Conversely, both AgNPs and CQDs@AgNPs displayed bactericidal activity. The distinction between bacteriostatic and bactericidal activity lies in their modes of action: bacteriostatic agents inhibit bacterial growth and reproduction without killing the bacteria, while bactericidal agents directly kill the bacteria [63]. In this case, both AgNPs and CQDs@AgNPs actively disrupt bacterial cell integrity and function, leading to cell death. Overall, this combination of CQDs and AgNPs significantly enhances antibacterial efficacy. However, the precise mechanisms underlying these effects remain complex and warrant further investigation. The CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites demonstrated the most potent and broad-spectrum antibacterial activity, likely due to the synergistic effects between AgNPs and positively charged CQDs, which promote ROS generation [19,62].

The DPPH assay was utilized to illustrate the decolorization of DPPH, which occurs due to a reduction in absorbance values in the presence of antioxidants. These antioxidants convert DPPH radicals into a yellow-colored compound, DPPH, with the degree of this reaction dependent on the hydrogen-donating capability of the antioxidants. Fig. 7 presents a dose-response bar chart displaying the percentage of DPPH radical scavenging activity for both AgNPs and CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites. The results indicate that both AgNPs and the CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites exhibit effective antioxidant properties, demonstrating inhibitory activity in a dose-dependent manner. Recent research highlights the significant potential of AgNPs in various biomedical applications. A study by Pirabbasi et al. demonstrates that AgNPs can function effectively as antioxidant agents [64], which enhances their antibacterial and wound healing properties. In our investigation, the CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites exhibited remarkable antioxidant activity against DPPH, achieving a maximum free radical scavenging rate of 63.90% at a concentration of 1000 μg/mL within 30 min, which significantly surpasses the 31.89% scavenging activity of AgNPs alone. This enhanced performance of the CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites can be attributed to the synergistic effects between CQDs and AgNPs, leading to improved oxidant integration on the surface of the nanocomposites, thereby amplifying their overall antioxidant efficacy.

Histological analysis was performed to assess the wound healing process after 9 days of treatment. As shown in Fig. 8 , on day 1, the incisional wounds in the untreated and CQDs@AgNPs groups were in the inflammatory phase of healing an increase in the density of inflammatory infiltrate with high infiltration of neutrophils to the wound site (indicated by a black triangle) was observed.

Additionally, newly formed cells were observed in the superficial horny layer (indicated by black arrows). On day 3, the untreated group and povidone iodine group exhibited disorganized granulation tissue compared to the treated group. Inflammatory infiltrates were present in untreated tissues, and the wounds were not fully filled. Additionally, newly formed cells were seen in the superficial horny layer (indicated by black arrows). The AgNPs, CQDs, and CQDs@AgNPs groups showed fewer inflammatory cells around the wound, likely due to their effective antibacterial properties. By day 6, the wounds had entered the proliferative phase of repair. The AgNPs, CQDs, and CQDs@AgNPs groups demonstrated enhanced re-epithelialization and granulation tissue formation, with notable fibroblast proliferation and angiogenesis in the CQDs@AgNPs-treated group (indicated by a black star), compared to the untreated and povidone iodine groups, which exhibited poor re-epithelialization and disorganized granulation tissue. Additionally, the CQDs@AgNPs group showed greater regularity in both the epithelium and connective tissue, as well as an increased number of fibroblasts, compared to the other groups. On day 9, the CQDs@AgNPs group demonstrated nearly complete epidermal differentiation, full re-epithelialization, and almost total wound closure (marked by a green star), whereas the other groups still exhibited open wounds. These results demonstrate that CQDs@AgNPs exhibited the most effective healing among the tested groups.

The study investigates the effects of AgNPs, CQDs, and CQDs@AgNPs on accelerating wound healing through in vivo experiments. Fig. 9 A present representative images of the healing process for the Untreated, Povidone iodine, AgNPs, CQDs, and CQDs@AgNPs groups on days 1, 3, 6, and 9. The results indicate that wounds treated with the CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites exhibited superior healing compared to the other groups, which still displayed noticeable defects after 9 days of treatment. Specifically, on day 3, all groups showed some degree of wound contraction, but the CQDs@AgNPs group demonstrated the highest wound closure rate at 57%. This suggests that the CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites had a significantly greater effect on promoting wound healing.

By day 6, clear epidermal regeneration was observed in the CQDs@AgNPs group, with further reduction in wound area in the other groups. However, no significant difference was noted between the CQDs and AgNPs groups, although both performed better than the povidone iodine and untreated groups. The wound closure rates on day 6 were 27% for the untreated group, 42% for povidone iodine, 68% for AgNPs, 72% for CQDs, and 77% for CQDs@AgNPs. By day 9, the CQDs@AgNPs group achieved a 92% wound healing rate, significantly outperforming the other groups (∗∗∗ P-value <0.001), which still had some unhealed areas. The analysis indicated that the CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites had the most effective healing properties, likely due to its strong antibacterial capabilities that help inhibit inflammation.

Recently, numerous studies have highlighted the significant impact of CQDs-based nanomaterials on wound healing. For example, a CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP nanocomposite demonstrated an impressive wound closure rate of 96.73 ± 1.25% within 12 days [44]. Similarly, the CS-CQD-TiO2-GO electrospun nanofibrous material achieved a notable 93.13% wound healing effect within 14 days [65]. Notably, our findings indicate that CQDs-decorated AgNPs derived from natural sources are highly effective in promoting accelerated wound closure, achieving comparable results in just 9 days.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance and comparison of the efficacy of CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites in wound healing and antibacterial activity with other carbon-based nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanostructures, such as graphene, GO, rGO, CNTs, GQDs, CQDs, g-C3N4, nanodiamonds, and fullerenes, have attracted considerable interest in biomedical fields. These carbon allotropes can be chemically modified or functionalized to enhance their properties, making them suitable candidates for applications in wound healing and antibacterial treatments. A comparative analysis of the efficacy of CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites in wound healing and antibacterial activity, relative to other carbon-based nanomaterials, is presented in Table 2 .

The comparative analysis underscores the pivotal role of CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites as potent antimicrobial agents against a broad spectrum of bacterial strains, including E. coli, S. aureus, C. albicans, A. baumannii, and K. pneumoniae. These nanocomposites exhibit a minimal inhibitory concentration of 117 μg/mL against E. coli, demonstrating their competitive antibacterial activity in comparison to other carbon-based nanomaterials. For instance, CuNPs@fullerenol presents MIC values of 1000 μg/mL against E. coli and S. aureus [70], positioning it as the second most effective option. In contrast, CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP demonstrate MICs of 1450 ± 260 μg/mL for E. coli and 1590 ± 120 μg/mL for S. aureus [44]. Other composites, such as ZnO/GQD, reports MIC values of 1600 μg/mL for E. coli and < 1600 μg/mL for S. aureus [72], while ZnO/GQD-PEI demonstrate MICs of 2000 μg/mL for E. coli [71]. In contrast, other studies reveal higher MIC values, such as FeCN-700/PMS, which shows MICs of 3000 μg/mL for E. coli and 10000 μg/mL for S. aureus [66], and CS/TiO2NPs/CQDs which exhibit MICs of 12500 μg/mL for E. coli and 6250 μg/mL for S. aureus [67]. Furthermore, CMC/CuONPs/CQDs presents substantially higher MIC values, reaching 25000 μg/mL for E. coli and over 50000 μg/mL for S. aureus [69]. The underlying mechanisms of action vary significantly among these materials; CQDs@AgNPs and FeCN-700/PMS primarily operate through electrostatic interactions, whereas CMC/CuONPs/CQDs penetrate bacterial cells, leading to cell death [69]. This illustrates the diverse strategies employed by these nanocomposites to exert their antimicrobial effects.

In addition to their antimicrobial properties, a comparative evaluation of the wound healing efficacy of CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites reveals their remarkable ability to accelerate wound closure, achieving a healing rate of 92% within just 9 days, thereby establishing them as one of the most effective wound healing agents available. In comparison, CuNPs@fullerenol demonstrates an 83.8% reduction in wound area over 7 days [70], indicating effective but comparatively slower performance. Notably, the CS/SF/N-CQDs/α-TCP nanocomposite exhibits an impressive wound closure rate of 96.73 ± 1.25%, albeit over a longer duration of 12 days. Collectively, these findings highlight the significant potential of CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites in wound healing applications, particularly due to their ability to deliver rapid and effective results.

In summary, this study presents the successful synthesis of CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites through a simple and eco-friendly chemical reduction approach, complemented by comprehensive characterization of their physicochemical properties. The results highlight the substantial promise CQDs@AgNPs for applications in wound healing and antibacterial therapies. Advanced characterization techniques such as SEM, FTIR, XRD, UV–Vis, and Zeta potential analysis, have provided vital insights into the structural and surface characteristics of these nanocomposites, elucidating the mechanisms underlying their remarkable efficacy. Importantly, CQDs@AgNPs showcase robust, broad-spectrum antibacterial activity against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacterial strains, with a MIC and MBC values of 0.117 mg/mL for E. coli, 3.75 mg/mL for K. pneumoniae, A. baumannii, and S. aureus, and 15 mg/mL for C. albicans. Additionally, the nanocomposites exhibited notable antioxidant activity of 63,90% toward DPPH, further enhancing its therapeutic potential. Notably, in vivo studies demonstrated excellent wound healing efficacy within 9 days, achieving a wound healing rate of 92%. Collectively, these findings position CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites as highly promising candidates for the development of advanced antimicrobial and wound healing agents. This research not only advances our understanding of nanocomposite materials but also paves the way for future clinical applications aimed at improving patient outcomes in healthcare.

This study introduces a novel approach for the synthesis of CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites, showcasing their significant potential to enhance wound healing and antibacterial efficacy. By incorporating both in vivo and in vitro models, the research offers a thorough evaluation of the nanocomposites’ effectiveness against clinically relevant bacterial strains. Advanced characterization techniques provide a detailed understanding of their properties, and the notable antioxidant activity observed further supports their multifaceted therapeutic potential in wound care. While these findings are promising, it is important to recognize several limitations. First, the in vivo experiments were conducted with a limited sample size, which may restrict the generalizability of the findings. Additionally, the study primarily focuses on short-term outcomes, highlighting the need for further research to assess long-term efficacy and safety. The mechanisms underlying the action of CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites also require deeper investigation to fully elucidate the molecular interactions involved. Furthermore, the study did not investigate the impact of varying dosages on therapeutic efficacy, which could yield important insights for optimizing treatment protocols.

While this study demonstrates the promising antimicrobial and wound healing capabilities of CQDs@AgNPs nanocomposites, several avenues warrant further exploration. Future research could focus on the long-term stability and biodegradability of these nanocomposites in physiological conditions to ensure their safety and efficacy in clinical settings. Additionally, extensive in vivo studies are necessary to validate the therapeutic potential observed in vitro and to better understand the mechanisms behind their antibacterial action and wound healing properties. Investigating the optimal dosage and application methods in diverse wound types could enhance their practical use in medical applications. Furthermore, exploring the scalability of the eco-friendly synthesis method and its application to other bio-waste materials could pave the way for sustainable practices in nanomaterial production. Overall, continuing to address these aspects will significantly contribute to the development of CQDs@AgNPs as viable candidates for advanced wound care and infection control.
